story_title: Zimbabwe's ZiG — The World's First Gold-Backed Digital Currency of the 21st Century
summary: On 5 April 2024, Zimbabwe introduced the Zimbabwe Gold (ZiG), a new currency backed by physical gold and foreign currency reserves held by the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe, replacing the Zimbabwe Gold (ZWG) and becoming the country's sixth attempt at a domestic currency since its hyperinflationary collapse in 2008.
recommended_arc: Hook: the country that printed a $100 trillion note now backs its currency with gold. Rising: Zimbabwe's five previous currencies all collapsed; its central bank bets gold will anchor the sixth. Climax: ZiG launches at 13.56 per US dollar, backed by 2.5 tonnes of physical gold. Payoff: within six months ZiG depreciates; gold backing proves insufficient without fiscal discipline.

verified_facts.claim: Zimbabwe introduced the Zimbabwe Gold (ZiG) on 5 April 2024, replacing the RTGS dollar/Zimbabwe dollar (ZWG); the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe set the initial exchange rate at 13.56 ZiG per 1 US dollar.

The Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe stated that at launch the ZiG was backed by a combination of 2.5 tonnes of physical gold reserves and approximately $100 million in foreign currency — giving the total reserve backing a value of about $285 million.

Zimbabwe's first gold-linked attempt was the Gold-Backed Digital Token (GBDT) launched in May 2023 as a store of value and means of payment, priced at the international spot price of gold; the ZiG superseded the GBDT as the national currency in April 2024.

The ZiG was Zimbabwe's sixth attempt at a domestic currency since the hyperinflationary crisis of 2007-2008 destroyed the Zimbabwe dollar, which reached an inflation rate of 79.6 billion percent per month in November 2008 — the second-highest inflation rate ever recorded.

By September 2024 — five months after the ZiG's launch — the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe devalued the ZiG by 43 percent against the US dollar, resetting the rate to approximately 24.4 ZiG per dollar, after persistent market pressure and a wide gap between the official rate and the parallel market rate.

Despite the devaluation, Zimbabwe's Reserve Bank governor John Mushayavanhu maintained that the ZiG remained fundamentally sound because its gold and foreign-currency backing had grown proportionally; the central bank reported gold reserves of approximately 1.4 tonnes after the devaluation.
